items.title: Replicative cycle
items.contentHtml:
Replicative cycle of Toxoplasma gondii

  • The cycle alternates between sexual reproduction (in enterocytes of the definitive host) and asexual (in tissues of intermediate hosts).
  • The sexual phase produces non-sporulated oocysts eliminated in feline feces; They sporulate in 1-5 days, becoming infective.
  • In the intermediate host, tachyzoites rapidly replicate in parasitophorous vacuoles, lysing cells and disseminating.
  • Under immune pressure, tachyzoites differentiate into bradyzoites within tissue cysts, mainly in muscle and CNS.

items.title: Definitive guest
items.contentHtml:
Definitive host: felines

  • The only hosts where the complete sexual cycle (gametogonia and sporogonia) occurs in enterocytes of the ileum.
  • Felines eliminate non-sporulated oocysts (10-100 million/day) during 1-3 weeks post-primary infection.
  • The sporulation requires oxygen and temperature 20-25°C; Sporulated oocysts are resistant to disinfectants and survive months in moist soil.
  • Cats become infected by ingesting prey tissue cysts (bradyzoites) or oocysts; there is no excretion in reinfections.

items.title: parasitic forms
items.contentHtml:
Forms: tachyzoite, bradyzoite, oocyst

  • Tachyzoite: rapid replicative form in acute phase; semilunar shape ~2-6 μm; invades any nucleated cell.
  • Bradyzoite: latent form in tissue cysts (thick wall); slow replication; responsible for chronic infection and reactivation.
  • Oocyst: environmental form; sporulated contains 2 sporocysts with 4 sporozoites each; resists acids and chlorine.
  • In immunocompromised patients, cyst rupture releases bradyzoites that become tachyzoites, causing reactivation.

items.foco: Transmission through raw meat and cat feces
items.title: Transmission
items.contentHtml:
Transmission through raw meat and cat feces

  • Ingestion of tissue cysts (bradyzoites) in raw or undercooked meat (pork, lamb, venison).
  • Ingestion of sporulated oocysts from cat feces (gardening, litter).
  • Transplacental transmission: tachyzoites cross the placenta; greater risk if acute maternal infection in 2nd-3rd trimester.
  • There is no direct person-to-person transmission (except transfusion/transplantation of infected organ).
  • Internal cooking at >67°C or freezing at -20°C for 24h inactivates cysts.

items.title: Congenital toxoplasmosis
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad of congenital toxoplasmosis

  • Chorioretinitis (bilateral in 85%), hydrocephalus (with periventricular calcifications) and intracranial calcifications.
  • Other manifestations: seizures, microcephaly, psychomotor retardation, hepatosplenomegaly, jaundice.
  • The complete triad occurs in <10% of infected neonates; the majority are asymptomatic at birth.
  • Prenatal diagnosis is confirmed with PCR in amniotic fluid after 18 weeks of gestation.
  • Treatment in pregnancy: spiramycin if there is no fetal infection; if there is fetal infection: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid.

items.title: ocular chorioretinitis
items.contentHtml:
Ocular toxoplasmosis chorioretinitis

  • Most common manifestation of late congenital infection; also due to reactivation in immunocompetent people.
  • Fund of the eye: yellowish-white retinal lesion (focal necrosis) with vitritis ("lighthouse in the fog") and cells in the anterior chamber.
  • Typical location: macula or near arcade vessels; It can be unilateral or bilateral, multifocal.
  • Clinical diagnosis; IgG/IgM serology and aqueous humor PCR if in doubt.
  • Treatment: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid + corticosteroids (if macula or optic nerve is threatened).

items.title: Cervical lymphadenitis
items.contentHtml:
Cervical lymphadenitis in immunocompetent people

  • More frequent presentation in immunocompetent adults; cervical lymphadenopathy (sometimes occipital, supraclavicular), painless, mobile, without suppuration.
  • It may be accompanied by fever, fatigue, myalgia; condition similar to infectious mononucleosis but without exudative pharyngitis.
  • Differential diagnosis: EBV, CMV, human immunodeficiency virus, lymphoma, tuberculosis.
  • Nodal biopsy shows histological triad: follicular hyperplasia, aggregates of epithelioid cells, and distortion sinus with mononuclear cells.
  • In immunocompetent patients it is usually self-limited; does not require antiparasitic treatment unless it persists >4 weeks or is severe.

items.foco: Ring brain lesions on computed tomography in human immunodeficiency virus
items.title: Neurotoxoplasmosis in human immunodeficiency virus
items.contentHtml:
Ring brain lesions on computed tomography in human immunodeficiency virus

  • In human immunodeficiency virus patients with CD4 count <100 cells/μL, reactivation of cerebral toxoplasmosis is the most common cause of lesion occupying intracranial space.
  • computed tomography with contrast: hypodense lesions with annular enhancement (ring), surrounded by vasogenic edema; typically multiple, location basal ganglia, thalamus, corticosubcortical junction.
  • Presumptive diagnosis: clinical and radiological response to empirical treatment in 10-14 days; if no improvement, consider primary CNS lymphoma or tuberculous abscess.
  • First-line treatment: pyrimethamine (200 mg load, then 50-75 mg/day) + sulfadiazine (4-6 g/day) + folinic acid (10-20 mg/day).

items.title: Serological diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis: IgG and IgM serology

  • Positive IgG indicates past infection; does not distinguish acute from chronic phase.
  • Positive IgM suggests recent infection (<4-8 weeks), but it can persist >12 months (false persistence).
  • To date infection in pregnancy, IgG avidity test is used: high avidity (>60%) rules out infection in the last 12-16 weeks; low (<30%) suggests recent infection.
  • In immunocompromised patients, serology may be negative; PCR in cerebrospinal fluid or brain biopsy is preferred.
  • IgA and IgE are used in neonatal diagnosis (IgM/IgA in cord blood).

items.title: Specific treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: pyrimethamine plus sulfadiazine

  • First-line regimen for active toxoplasmosis (ocular, cerebral, congenital, disseminated).
  • Pyrimethamine (dihydrofolate reductase inhibitor) + sulfadiazine (dihydropteroate synthetase inhibitor) act synergistically in the folate pathway.
  • Always add folinic acid (leucovorin) 10-20 mg/day to prevent myelosuppression by pyrimethamine.
  • Adult dose cerebral toxoplasmosis: pyrimethamine 200 mg PO load, then 50-75 mg/day; sulfadiazine 4-6 g/day PO in 4 divided doses.
  • Alternative in those allergic to sulfa: clindamycin 600 mg intravenously/6h + pyrimethamine; or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (15-20 mg/kg/day TMP) as another option.

items.foco: Prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole in human immunodeficiency virus
items.title: human immunodeficiency virus prophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole in human immunodeficiency virus

  • Indication: human immunodeficiency virus patients with CD4 <100 cells/μL and positive IgG serology for Toxoplasma.
  • Regimen of choice: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) 1 double strong tablet (160/800 mg) every 24 hours or 3 times a week.
  • Alternative if sulfa allergy: dapsone 50 mg/day + pyrimethamine 50 mg/week + folinic acid; or atovaquone 1500 mg/day.
  • Prophylaxis is suspended when CD4 >200 cells/μL for >3 months with stable ART.
  • Primoinfection in human immunodeficiency virus requires treating acute phase and then lifelong secondary prophylaxis if CD4 does not recover.

items.foco: Avoid contact with cat feces during pregnancy
items.title: Prevention in pregnancy
items.contentHtml:
Avoid contact with cat feces during pregnancy

  • Seronegative pregnant women should avoid cleaning litter boxes; if unavoidable, use gloves and wash hands.
  • Consume only well-cooked meat (>67°C internally); avoid raw sausages (serrano ham, salami).
  • Wash fruits and vegetables; avoid gardening without gloves (possible contamination with oocysts).
  • If routine serology is performed, negative IgG indicates susceptibility; repeat every 8-12 weeks until delivery.
  • In case of seroconversion during pregnancy, refer to maternal-fetal medicine to evaluate treatment and possible prenatal diagnosis.
